quote: Neil. I'm here at the moment. This is Lawanda. What can I do for you?
stewart_read: The rep is immediately told the requestor (Neil) is not on the line and that a proxy (Lawanda) has answered. The rep never asks to speak with Neil, never establishes Lawanda's relationship to Neil or to the property, and never determines whether Lawanda is on the title. The entire qualification sequence — including the eventual credit DQ — is conducted with the wrong person. Strategic read: the rep may have judged that Lawanda could relay information and that pressing for Neil would create friction. Reactive read: the rep defaulted to whoever answered and never course-corrected. The reactive read holds — there is no evidence of a deliberate strategy to qualify through Lawanda and then route to Neil.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep deliberately qualified through Lawanda as a relay to avoid friction, intending to ask for Neil before booking.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk the rep through the verify.title_co_owners checkpoint as a proxy-detection moment: when someone other than the named requestor answers, the first move is to establish the relationship and ask for the requestor before proceeding. A suggested redirect: 'Hey Lawanda, is Neil available? I want to make sure I'm talking to the right person since the request came in under his name.' This is not about being rude to Lawanda — it's about not DQ'ing a lead based on a proxy's self-reported credit estimate.

stewart_read: The rep captures roof age (approximately 15 years) and acknowledges it with a neutral affirmation. This is a micro-momentum moment: the roof clears the threshold, which is a positive qualification signal. The rep had an opportunity to briefly mark this as a green light — building forward value before the credit gate — but moved directly to the credit qualifier instead. The acknowledgment is not wrong; it is simply flat where it could have been a small energy builder.
coaching_implication: Coach the rep to treat each cleared qualifier as a brief positive anchor before moving to the next gate. A suggested beat: 'Okay, fifteen years — that works well for us. Let me ask you a couple more quick things.' This costs nothing and keeps the customer's energy up heading into the credit question.

quote: The main requirements for solar are just that you own your home and have a credit score above 650. Is that the case for you?
stewart_read: Two issues compound here. First, the rep states the credit threshold as 650, not 670 — a schema drift that has now appeared across multiple sessions (SESSION18_fcbf33c1 also cited 650). This is a floor-wide calibration issue, not an individual rep error. Second, the rep reaches the credit gate after completing only a partial verify: address, home type, and roof age were captured, but bill amount, utility company, interest in solar, and title/co-owners were skipped. Jumping to the credit qualifier before completing verify means the rep has no bill anchor, no emotional context, and no co-applicant information — all of which would have been relevant to handling the credit uncertainty that followed.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been following a compressed script variant that front-loads the hard DQ gates to avoid wasting time on unqualifiable leads.
coaching_implication: Two separate coaching beats. (1) Credit threshold: the manager needs to resolve the 650 vs 670 discrepancy floor-wide — this is the second documented instance. Until resolved, every rep is potentially DQ'ing leads at the wrong number. (2) Verify sequence: the rep should complete bill, utility, and interest-in-solar before reaching the credit gate. Those fields provide the emotional context that makes the credit question land softer and give the rep something to work with if the answer is uncertain.

quote: Do you have a way to check your credit score?
stewart_read: The rep shows the instinct to probe rather than accept the first uncertain answer — this is a partial-credit execution. However, the probe is directed at Lawanda, who is not the requestor and cannot check Neil's credit. The rep had the right impulse (don't accept 'I don't think so' as a final answer) but applied it to the wrong person. A more productive probe would have been to ask for Neil directly at this point, or to ask whether Neil might have a different credit profile.
coaching_implication: Reinforce the instinct to probe — the rep did not simply accept the first soft DQ, which is good. The coaching point is to redirect the probe: 'Is Neil available? He might have a better sense of his own score, and it's worth a quick check.' This keeps the lead alive without fabricating a path that doesn't exist.

quote: Yeah. If you wanna look into that or if Neil's credit score is 650 or above, we'd be able to help you out.
stewart_read: The rep surfaces Neil's name here — acknowledging that Neil is the actual requestor — but does not ask to speak with Neil or schedule a callback with Neil specifically. The rep has the information needed to route correctly (Neil is the requestor, Lawanda is a proxy) and does not use it. This is the clearest single moment where the call's outcome was determined: the rep had the name, had the opening, and issued a passive invitation instead of a locked next step.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that Lawanda would relay the information to Neil and that a direct ask for Neil would feel presumptuous.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through the difference between a passive callback invitation and a locked next step. A suggested alternative: 'Is Neil around? I'd love to take two minutes with him directly — it'll save you both a call.' If Neil is not available, the move is to schedule a specific callback time with Neil, not to issue an open-ended invitation. The schema's multi_angle_resilience principle applies: the rep had at least two more angles available (ask for Neil, schedule a locked callback) and used neither.

quote: Feel free to call this number back, though.
stewart_read: The rep closes with a passive, open-ended callback invitation rather than a locked time. Per the schema's soft_outcome_distinction directive, callbacks with locked times have materially different show rates than 'we'll touch base' closings. This is the latter. There is no date, no time, no confirmation that Neil will be the one calling, and no follow-up text promised. The lead exits the call with no structural anchor.
coaching_implication: Even in a soft-DQ scenario, the rep should close with a locked next step if any path forward exists. Here, the path was clear: Neil is the requestor, Neil's credit is unknown, Neil should be on the call. The button_up protocol — date, time, phone confirmation, 'is that fair' handshake — applies to callbacks as much as to appointments. At minimum: 'When's a good time to reach Neil directly? I can call back at a specific time that works for him.'

quote: And then if it's not 650, once it gets to that point, we'd be more than happy to run a quote for you.
stewart_read: The rep offers a future path (call back when credit improves) but frames it around the wrong threshold (650 vs 670) and directs it at Lawanda rather than Neil. This compounds the schema drift identified at 01:20. The rep is now making a forward promise — 'we'd be more than happy to run a quote' — based on a credit number that may not match Ion's actual policy. If Neil calls back at 655 expecting to qualify, the floor will have a credibility problem.
coaching_implication: The 650 vs 670 discrepancy needs floor-wide resolution before this rep (or any rep) makes forward promises tied to the threshold. Until the manager resolves this with Kenny, the coaching note is: do not quote a specific credit number in a forward promise. A safer close: 'Have Neil give us a call and we'll take a look at what options are available for him.'
